name: Compression Fitting

aliases.name: Olive fitting
aliases.context: UK trade term — refers to the brass olive inside

aliases.name: Compression ring fitting
aliases.context: Descriptive term used in training materials

description: A mechanical fitting that joins two pipes or a pipe to a fixture using a compression nut, a brass olive (ring), and a fitting body. Tightening the nut compresses the olive against the pipe, creating a watertight seal without soldering.
useCases: Joining copper pipes in domestic plumbing
Repairs where soldering is not possible (e.g., near timber or insulation)
installationNotes: Do not overtighten — typically 1 to 1.25 turns past hand-tight. Use PTFE tape or jointing compound on the olive for extra security. Always use pipe inserts when connecting to plastic pipe.

name: Push-Fit Fitting

aliases.name: Speedfit
aliases.context: JG Speedfit brand name — widely used generically in UK

aliases.name: SharkBite
aliases.context: Major US brand name — often used generically

description: A fitting that connects pipes by simply pushing the pipe into the fitting body. Internal grab ring teeth grip the pipe, while an O-ring creates the seal. No tools, solder, or compression nuts required. Can be removed and reused.
useCases: Fast repairs and joins without heat or tools
Tight spaces where a wrench can't reach
installationNotes: Ensure pipe is cut square and deburred. Push pipe in until it hits the internal stop — mark the insertion depth on the pipe first. Pipe inserts are required for plastic pipes. To disconnect, push the release collar toward the fitting and pull the pipe out.

name: Gate Valve

aliases.name: Full-bore valve
aliases.context: Describes the unrestricted flow when fully open

aliases.name: Wedge valve
aliases.context: Engineering term referring to internal gate shape

description: A valve that controls flow by raising or lowering an internal gate (wedge) via a handwheel. When fully open, provides unrestricted full-bore flow. Designed for fully open or fully closed positions — not for throttling or regulating flow.
useCases: Main water shut-off for a building
Isolating sections of a plumbing system
installationNotes: Gate valves should only be used fully open or fully closed. Using one partially open will cause the gate to erode and the valve to fail. Replace with a ball valve if throttling is needed. Operate the handwheel periodically to prevent seizing.

name: Ball Valve

aliases.name: Lever valve
aliases.context: Refers to the lever handle style

description: A valve with a hollow, perforated ball inside that pivots 90° to open or close flow. The lever handle makes operation fast — a quarter turn from fully open to fully closed. Offers reliable, tight shut-off and is widely used as the modern replacement for gate valves.
useCases: Isolating water supply to fixtures or appliances
Gas line shut-off (yellow handle, gas-rated models only)
installationNotes: Red or blue handles indicate hot/cold water. Yellow handles indicate gas-rated valves — never use a water-rated ball valve on gas. Lever should be parallel to pipe when open, perpendicular when closed.

name: Stop Valve

aliases.name: Stopcock
aliases.context: UK term — often refers to the main stop valve

description: A valve that stops or starts flow using a washer pressed against a seat by a screw-down mechanism. The handwheel is turned multiple times to fully open or close. Often found under sinks and behind toilets for local fixture isolation.
useCases: Isolating individual fixtures (sinks, toilets, washing machines)
Main stopcock for a domestic property
installationNotes: The washer inside will wear over time and can be replaced without removing the valve body. Arrow on the body indicates flow direction — install accordingly. The main stopcock is typically located where the mains supply enters the building.

name: P-Trap

aliases.name: Waste trap
aliases.context: General term for any drain trap

aliases.name: Bottle trap
aliases.context: A compact variant of the same function — cylindrical body

aliases.name: U-bend
aliases.context: Older colloquial term (technically describes an S-trap)

description: A curved section of pipe beneath a drain that retains a small amount of water, creating a seal that prevents sewer gases from entering the building. The 'P' shape refers to the profile when viewed from the side. Every plumbed fixture must have a trap.
connectionType: Compression waste / Solvent weld / Push-fit waste
compatibility: Basin, sink, bath, and shower outlets
useCases: Under every sink, basin, bath, and shower
Connecting fixtures to the waste drainage system
Preventing sewer gas entry into occupied spaces
installationNotes: Ensure the trap is properly filled with water after installation — run the tap. If a fixture is unused for long periods, the water seal can evaporate (pour water in periodically). The trap depth (water seal) should be at least 50mm. Clean regularly to prevent blockages.

name: Float Valve

aliases.name: Ball cock
aliases.context: Traditional term — still widely used in UK trade

aliases.name: Ballvalve
aliases.context: Alternative spelling used in British standards

aliases.name: Toilet fill valve
aliases.context: US term for the cistern variant

description: A valve that automatically controls water level in a tank or cistern. A buoyant float rises with the water level and, through a lever arm or piston mechanism, shuts off the inlet when the desired level is reached. Found in toilet cisterns, cold water storage tanks, and header tanks.
useCases: Filling toilet cisterns to correct level
Maintaining water level in loft storage tanks
Feed and expansion tank for heating systems
installationNotes: Adjust the float arm or screw to set the correct water level — water should be about 25mm below the overflow. Part 1 valves are for high-pressure mains; Part 2 valves are for low-pressure tank-fed systems. Check the washer if the valve drips or doesn't shut off fully.

name: Check Valve
description: A valve that allows fluid to flow in one direction only. An internal flap, disc, or spring mechanism opens with forward flow and closes automatically if flow reverses, preventing backflow contamination. Required by water regulations at many connection points.
useCases: Preventing backflow from a heating system into mains water
At the outlet of booster pumps
Where water regulations require backflow protection (e.g., filling loops)
installationNotes: Install with the arrow on the body pointing in the direction of desired flow. Double-check valves are required where the risk category is higher. Must be testable and accessible for inspection in commercial installations.

name: Flexible Tap Connector

aliases.name: Braided hose
aliases.context: Describes the stainless steel braid exterior

description: A flexible, braided stainless steel hose with fittings on each end, used to connect water supply pipes to taps, toilets, and appliances. Eliminates the need for precise pipe alignment and simplifies installation in tight spaces.
useCases: Appliance connections in kitchens and utility rooms
installationNotes: Do not kink or twist the hose during installation — route gently. Check for leaks immediately after turning on supply. Replace every 5–10 years or immediately if any bulging, cracking, or corrosion is visible on the braid. Always use an isolating valve upstream.

name: Pipe Clip

aliases.name: Munsen ring
aliases.context: Heavy-duty split ring type for larger pipes

aliases.name: Saddle clip
aliases.context: Describes the shape of some metal clips

description: A bracket or clip used to secure pipes to walls, floors, joists, or other structural surfaces. Keeps pipework neat, supported, and prevents movement or stress on joints. Available in plastic snap-in, metal saddle, and heavy-duty split ring styles.
useCases: Supporting horizontal pipe runs to prevent sagging
Fixing pipes in place before plastering or boxing in
installationNotes: Space clips at recommended intervals: 1.2m for horizontal 15mm copper, 1.8m for vertical. Use the correct size — a loose clip won't support the pipe. Allow for thermal expansion on long runs of plastic pipe (use slide clips, not fixed). Use plastic clips on plastic pipe to avoid damage.

name: Isolation Valve

aliases.name: Screwdriver valve
aliases.context: Describes the slotted head operated by a screwdriver

description: A small inline valve used to isolate the water supply to an individual fixture or appliance without shutting off the entire system. Operated by turning the slotted head 90° with a screwdriver or coin — no handwheel required.
useCases: Isolating a single tap for washer replacement
Shutting off water to a toilet for maintenance
Installed on each supply line to every fixture in a modern installation
installationNotes: Slot parallel to pipe = open. Slot perpendicular to pipe = closed. Building regulations now require isolation valves on the supply to every fixture. Compact size allows installation in tight spaces under basins and behind toilets.

name: Copper Elbow

aliases.name: Street elbow
aliases.context: Has one male and one female end — fits directly into another fitting

aliases.name: Sweep elbow
aliases.context: Long-radius elbow for gentler flow change

aliases.name: Solder ring elbow
aliases.context: Yorkshire fitting — pre-loaded solder ring inside

description: A fitting that changes the direction of a pipe run by 90° (or 45° in angled variants). Available as end-feed (requires solder), solder-ring (pre-loaded solder), or compression types. One of the most commonly used fittings in domestic plumbing.
useCases: Changing pipe direction around corners and obstacles
Routing pipe runs through walls and floors
installationNotes: Clean pipe and fitting with wire wool before soldering. Apply flux evenly. For solder-ring fittings, heat evenly until a ring of solder appears around the joint. Let cool without disturbing. Compression elbows are used where heat is not safe (near timber, insulation).

name: Tee Fitting

aliases.name: Branch tee
aliases.context: Describes its function of creating a branch line

aliases.name: Equal tee
aliases.context: All three ports are the same size

aliases.name: Reducing tee
aliases.context: Branch port is smaller than the run ports

description: A T-shaped fitting that creates a branch connection from a main pipe run. Used to split or combine flow. Available as equal tees (all ports same size) or reducing tees (branch is a smaller diameter). Available in solder, compression, and push-fit types.
useCases: Branching a hot or cold supply to a new fixture
installationNotes: When soldering, feed all three joints at once — heat the fitting body, not the pipe. On a live system, use a compression or push-fit tee to avoid draining down. Ensure flow direction is correct on reducing tees.

name: Pipe Reducer

aliases.name: Bell reducer
aliases.context: US term for the concentric shape

description: A fitting that connects two pipes of different diameters, allowing a transition between pipe sizes. Concentric reducers keep the centerline aligned; eccentric reducers offset the centerline (used to prevent air pockets in horizontal runs).
useCases: Transitioning from a 22mm main run to 15mm branch supply
Connecting older large-diameter pipework to modern smaller sizes
Reducing pipe size near terminal fixtures
installationNotes: Always reduce in the direction of flow where possible. Use eccentric reducers on horizontal runs to keep the bottom of the pipe flush and avoid air locks. Available in solder-ring and end-feed types.

name: Schrader Valve

aliases.name: Charging valve
aliases.context: Used when adding refrigerant to a system

aliases.name: Tire valve
aliases.context: Same valve mechanism used on car/bike tires

description: A spring-loaded valve core inside a threaded port, providing a sealed access point for connecting gauges and charging refrigerant into HVAC systems. The same mechanism used in tire valves. A cap must always be in place when not in use to prevent leaks.
useCases: Connecting manifold gauges for pressure readings
installationNotes: Always replace the cap after service — the cap is the primary seal, not the valve core. Use a Schrader core removal tool if the core needs replacement. Depressing the pin without a hose connected will release refrigerant (EPA violation).

name: Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)

aliases.name: Metering device
aliases.context: Functional category — TXV is one type of metering device

description: A precision metering device that regulates the flow of liquid refrigerant into the evaporator based on the superheat at the evaporator outlet. A sensing bulb attached to the suction line detects temperature changes and adjusts the valve opening to maintain optimal superheat.
installationNotes: Mount the sensing bulb at 10 or 2 o'clock on the suction line (never at the bottom — oil collects there). Insulate the bulb to prevent false readings from ambient air. Superheat adjustment screw: clockwise = more superheat (less refrigerant), counterclockwise = less superheat.

name: Filter Drier

aliases.name: Liquid line drier
aliases.context: Describes its location in the system

aliases.name: Moisture indicator
aliases.context: Some models include a sight glass with moisture indicator

description: An inline device on the liquid line that removes moisture and particulate contaminants from the refrigerant. Contains a desiccant core (molecular sieve) that absorbs water, and a filter screen that traps debris. Protects the TXV and compressor from damage.
sizes: ½" OD (matched to liquid line size)
useCases: Installed on every AC and refrigeration system liquid line
Replaced after any compressor burnout (critical — contaminated oil)
Replaced after system has been opened to atmosphere
installationNotes: Arrow on the body indicates flow direction — install with flow from condenser toward TXV. Replace every time the system is opened for major repair. Overheating during brazing can damage the desiccant core — use a wet rag as a heat sink. Always pull a deep vacuum after replacement.

name: Air Handler Unit

aliases.name: Indoor unit
aliases.context: In split system context — the part inside the building

aliases.name: Blower unit
aliases.context: Colloquial term focusing on the fan component

description: The indoor component of a split HVAC system containing the blower fan, evaporator coil, air filter, and sometimes supplemental electric heat strips. Draws return air from the building, passes it over the coil (for cooling or heating), and distributes conditioned air through the ductwork.
compatibility: Matched outdoor condensing unit (same brand/AHRI rating)
useCases: Paired with heat pump or AC condensing unit
installationNotes: Must be matched to the outdoor unit by AHRI certification for rated efficiency. Condensate drain must be properly trapped and routed. Filter should be accessible for regular replacement. Install on a drain pan in attic locations to catch condensate overflow.

name: Damper

aliases.name: Fire damper
aliases.context: Safety type — closes automatically in a fire

aliases.name: VAV box
aliases.context: Variable Air Volume — motorized damper with controller

description: A device installed inside ductwork that regulates or blocks airflow. Manual dampers use a lever to adjust a blade; motorized dampers are controlled by thermostats or building automation systems. Fire dampers are safety devices that close automatically when heat-activated.
useCases: Fire safety in commercial ductwork penetrations
installationNotes: Manual dampers: lever parallel to duct = open, perpendicular = closed. Fire dampers must be accessible for inspection — install access doors. Motorized dampers need 24V power and control signal from thermostat or BAS. Label all dampers clearly for future technicians.

name: Condenser Fan Motor

aliases.name: Universal condenser motor
aliases.context: Aftermarket replacement that fits multiple units

description: The electric motor that drives the fan blade on top of (or beside) the outdoor condensing unit. Draws ambient air across the condenser coil to reject heat from the refrigerant. Failure causes high head pressure and system shutdown on safety.
compatibility: Specific to condensing unit model (check OEM part number)
Universal motors available for common HP/RPM/rotation specs
useCases: Replacement when motor fails (seized bearings, burned windings)
installationNotes: Match HP, RPM, rotation direction, shaft diameter, and voltage to original motor. Wire the run capacitor correctly — wrong capacitance will damage the motor. Note rotation direction before removing old motor. Disconnect power at the breaker before starting work.

name: Expansion Vessel
description: A sealed vessel with an internal rubber diaphragm that absorbs the expansion of water as it heats in a sealed heating system. One side contains pre-charged air/nitrogen; the other side connects to the system water. Prevents dangerous pressure buildup and frequent pressure relief valve discharge.
useCases: Every sealed heating system requires one
Replacement when pressure drops repeatedly (failed diaphragm)
Additional vessel when system has been extended
installationNotes: Pre-charge pressure must match the system cold fill pressure (typically 1.0–1.5 bar). Check and adjust with a dry tire gauge on the Schrader valve before filling the system. If the vessel is waterlogged (water comes from the Schrader valve), the diaphragm has failed — replace the vessel.

name: Circulator Pump

aliases.name: Zone pump
aliases.context: When used for a single zone

aliases.name: Wet rotor pump
aliases.context: Describes the motor design — rotor runs in the system water

aliases.name: Grundfos pump
aliases.context: Major brand name — often used generically (like 'Hoover' for vacuum)

description: An electrically powered pump that circulates hot water through a central heating system (from boiler through radiators/underfloor heating and back). Wet rotor design means the motor is cooled and lubricated by the system water flowing through it.
useCases: Pumping hot water through radiator circuits
Zoned heating systems (one pump per zone)
installationNotes: Install with the motor shaft horizontal (not vertical) to prevent bearing wear. Bleed air from the pump via the bleed screw after filling. Modern pumps (e.g., Grundfos UPS2, UPS3) auto-detect speed — older pumps have manual speed settings (1, 2, 3). Isolator valves on both sides for easy replacement.

name: Pressure Relief Valve

aliases.name: PRV
aliases.context: Common abbreviation (can also mean Pressure Reducing Valve — context matters)

aliases.name: Safety valve
aliases.context: Functional term emphasizing its safety role

aliases.name: T&P valve
aliases.context: Temperature & Pressure relief valve — US term for water heater version

description: A spring-loaded safety valve that opens automatically when system pressure exceeds a set limit (typically 3 bar for domestic boilers), releasing water to prevent dangerous over-pressurization. The discharge must be piped to a safe, visible location. This is a critical safety device.
compatibility: Unvented hot water cylinders (set at 6 or 7 bar)
useCases: Mandatory safety device on every sealed heating system
Discharge indicates a system fault (failed expansion vessel, overfilling)
installationNotes: NEVER cap, plug, or block the discharge from a PRV — it is a life-safety device. Discharge pipe must fall continuously to a visible, safe location outside the building. If the PRV is frequently discharging, investigate the cause: expansion vessel failure, filling loop left open, or system overfilled. Replace the PRV — do not attempt to repair it.

name: Zone Valve

aliases.name: Motorized valve
aliases.context: General term for any motor-driven valve

aliases.name: Mid-position valve
aliases.context: UK term — 3-port valve that can serve heating, hot water, or both

aliases.name: Honeywell valve
aliases.context: Dominant brand — V4043H (2-port) and V4073A (3-port) are industry standard

description: A motorized valve that controls the flow of hot water to different zones in a central heating system (e.g., radiators vs. hot water cylinder). Controlled by the programmer/timer and room thermostat. The motor opens the valve; a spring returns it to closed.
useCases: Directing heating to radiators when room thermostat calls for heat
Directing hot water to the cylinder when cylinder thermostat calls
S-Plan: separate 2-port valve for each zone
installationNotes: The motor head can be replaced without draining the system — unclip and swap. When the valve reaches fully open, a microswitch fires to tell the boiler to ignite. Check the microswitch if the boiler won't fire. Manual lever on top allows opening for filling/draining without power.

name: Heat Exchanger

aliases.name: Plate heat exchanger
aliases.context: Most common type in domestic boilers

aliases.name: Brazed plate
aliases.context: Describes construction — stainless steel plates brazed together

aliases.name: Primary heat exchanger
aliases.context: The main burner-to-water exchanger in a boiler

aliases.name: Secondary heat exchanger
aliases.context: In combi boilers — heats DHW from primary circuit

description: A device that transfers heat between two fluids without mixing them. In boilers, the primary heat exchanger transfers heat from the gas burner flame to the heating water. In combi boilers, a secondary plate heat exchanger heats domestic hot water from the primary heating water.
sizes: Replacement must match boiler model exactly
compatibility: Specific to boiler make and model
useCases: Core component of every boiler — transfers combustion heat to water
Secondary DHW heating in combi boilers
External plate heat exchangers for system separation
installationNotes: Scaling and sludge are the main enemies — a clean system with inhibitor protects the heat exchanger. Kettling noise (rumbling/banging) usually indicates scale buildup on the heat exchanger. Flushing the system with a powerflush machine can restore performance. Replacement is model-specific and often requires significant boiler disassembly.

name: Lockshield Valve

aliases.name: Balancing valve
aliases.context: Functional term — used to balance the system

description: A valve on one end of a radiator (opposite the TRV or manual valve) used to balance the heating system. Has a protective cap that must be removed to access the adjusting spindle. Set during commissioning to ensure each radiator heats at the correct rate relative to others.
useCases: Balancing a central heating system so all radiators heat evenly
Partially closing to restrict flow to radiators nearest the pump
Draining a radiator (close the other valve, open the lockshield with a bucket)
installationNotes: Balancing procedure: open all lockshields fully, then progressively close those on radiators nearest the pump. Measure the temperature difference between flow and return at each radiator — aim for 11–12°C difference across each radiator. Use an adjustable spanner or lockshield key to turn the spindle.

name: Thermostatic Radiator Valve (TRV)

aliases.name: Room thermostat valve
aliases.context: Describes its role in room temperature control

description: A self-regulating valve fitted to a radiator that controls room temperature by adjusting water flow based on the surrounding air temperature. A wax or liquid-filled sensor in the valve head expands and contracts to open or close the valve pin — no electrical power needed.
compatibility: Standard M30 × 1.5 head connection (most European brands)
useCases: Energy saving — reduces flow to radiators in warm rooms
Required by building regulations on most radiators in new installations
installationNotes: Do NOT fit a TRV in the same room as the room thermostat — they will conflict. Fit on the flow side of the radiator (lockshield on the return). Keep the TRV head away from heat sources and curtains that could give false readings. Smart TRV heads are available that replace the mechanical head with app-controlled versions.

name: Filling Loop

aliases.name: Bypass link
aliases.context: Sometimes confused — a bypass is different

aliases.name: Keyless filling loop
aliases.context: Modern type that doesn't require a key/lever

description: A short braided hose or pipe assembly that temporarily connects the mains cold water supply to the sealed heating system for filling and pressurizing. Includes one or two isolation valves and often a check valve to prevent heating water contaminating the mains supply.
compatibility: Combi boilers (many have a built-in filling loop)
System boilers with external filling loop
useCases: Initial fill and pressurization of a new heating system
Topping up pressure when the gauge drops below 1 bar
installationNotes: Water regulations require the filling loop to be DISCONNECTABLE — do not permanently connect the mains to a heating system. Fill to 1.0–1.5 bar cold (check boiler manual). Close both valves AND disconnect the hose after filling. If pressure drops frequently, check for leaks or a failed expansion vessel.

name: 1/2" PVC Schedule 40 Cap
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 pressure cap that fits over the end of a 1/2" PVC pipe to seal it. Socket (slip) connection — glued onto the pipe end using PVC cement. Used to terminate unused pipe runs or for pressure testing.
useCases: Temporary or permanent termination of a pipe run
installationNotes: Clean both the pipe end and inside of the cap with PVC primer before applying PVC cement. Apply cement to both surfaces and push together with a quarter turn. Hold for 10 seconds. Allow full cure time before pressurizing.

name: 1/2" PVC Schedule 40 Tee SxSxFPT

aliases.name: Female tee
aliases.context: Refers to the FPT (Female Pipe Thread) branch

aliases.name: Reducing tee
aliases.context: When branch differs from run size

description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 tee fitting with two socket (slip) ends and one female pipe thread (FPT) branch. Allows branching a pipe run and connecting to a threaded component on the branch outlet.
useCases: Branching a supply line to a threaded valve or fixture
Creating distribution points in irrigation systems
Connecting threaded accessories to a PVC pipe run
installationNotes: Solvent weld the socket ends. Use PTFE tape on any threaded connections. Do not overtighten threaded joints in PVC — plastic threads are more fragile than metal.

name: 3/4" PVC Schedule 40 Cap
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 pressure cap that fits over the end of a 3/4" PVC pipe to seal it. Socket (slip) connection — glued onto the pipe end using PVC cement.
useCases: Temporary or permanent termination of a pipe run
installationNotes: Use PVC primer and cement. Push together with a quarter turn and hold for 10 seconds.

name: 3/4" PVC Schedule 40 Plug MPT
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 plug with male pipe thread (MPT). Screws into a female-threaded fitting to seal the opening. Used to cap off threaded outlets in PVC piping systems.
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to the male threads before screwing in. Hand-tighten plus one turn with a wrench — do not overtighten PVC threads.

name: 3/4" × 1/2" PVC Reducer Bushing
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 reducer bushing that steps down from 3/4" to 1/2". The spigot end fits into a 3/4" fitting socket, and the internal socket accepts a 1/2" pipe. Allows transition between pipe sizes within a fitting.
useCases: Reducing pipe size within an existing fitting
Transitioning from larger to smaller supply lines
Adapting fittings when exact size matches aren't available
installationNotes: Apply PVC primer and cement to both the bushing exterior and the receiving fitting socket. Insert fully and hold.

name: 3/4" PVC Schedule 40 Male Adapter
description: A PVC Schedule 40 male adapter with a socket (slip) end and a male pipe thread (MPT) end. Allows transitioning from a solvent-weld PVC system to a threaded connection for valves, metal fittings, or equipment.
useCases: Connecting PVC pipe to a threaded valve
Transitioning from PVC to metal piping
installationNotes: Solvent weld the socket end to PVC pipe. Apply PTFE tape to the male threads before connecting to threaded fittings. Do not over-tighten.

name: 1/2" Viega ProPress Cap
description: A Viega ProPress 1/2" copper cap (CA-01) for sealing the end of a copper pipe run using press-fit technology. No soldering required — crimped onto the pipe with a pressing tool for a permanent, watertight seal.
compatibility: 1/2" copper pipe (Type K, L, or M)
useCases: Capping off copper pipe ends in hydronic heating systems
Quick repairs without soldering near combustible materials
installationNotes: Mark the insertion depth on the pipe. Clean and deburr the pipe end. Insert pipe fully into the fitting and press with the appropriate jaw size. A green dot on Viega fittings indicates it has NOT been pressed — the dot disappears after pressing.

name: 1/2" Viega ProPress Coupling
description: A Viega ProPress 1/2" copper coupling that joins two pieces of copper pipe using press-fit technology. No solder, flux, or open flame required. Ideal for boiler and heating system installations where speed and fire safety matter.
compatibility: 1/2" copper pipe (Type K, L, or M)
useCases: Joining copper pipe sections in boiler piping
Repairs in confined spaces where soldering isn't safe
installationNotes: Deburr and clean pipe ends. Mark insertion depth. Press both ends with the correct jaw size. Verify the green dot is gone on both sides after pressing.

name: 3/4" Viega ProPress Adapter
description: A Viega ProPress 3/4" adapter (AD-02) that transitions from a press-fit copper connection to a male NPT threaded connection. Allows connecting ProPress copper piping to threaded valves, boilers, and equipment without soldering.
compatibility: 3/4" FPT fittings, valves, and equipment (threaded end)
useCases: Connecting ProPress copper piping to a boiler
Transitioning to threaded zone valves or circulators
installationNotes: Press the copper end first, then thread into the equipment. Apply PTFE tape or thread sealant to the male threads. Do not use a pipe wrench on the copper press portion.

name: 1/2" Copper 45° Elbow CxC
description: An Everbilt 1/2" copper 45° elbow with CxC (cup × cup / sweat × sweat) connections. Changes pipe direction by 45 degrees with a gentler turn than a 90° elbow, reducing flow restriction. Joined by soldering.
compatibility: 1/2" copper pipe (Type K, L, or M)
useCases: Gradual direction changes in copper piping
Routing around obstacles with less flow restriction than a 90°
Water supply and drainage vent piping
installationNotes: Clean pipe and fitting interior with emery cloth. Apply flux, assemble, and solder. The gentler angle creates less friction loss than a 90° elbow.

name: 1/2" Copper 90° Street Elbow C×FTG
description: An Everbilt 1/2" copper 90° street elbow with one cup (female/sweat) end and one FTG (male/fitting) end. The male end fits directly into another fitting socket without requiring an additional piece of pipe, saving space and materials.
useCases: Tight spaces where a standard elbow + pipe stub won't fit
Connecting directly into another fitting (e.g., tee, valve)
Reducing the number of solder joints in a run
installationNotes: The FTG end inserts directly into a fitting socket — no pipe stub needed. Clean and flux both ends. Useful for compact manifold assemblies.

name: 1/2" PVC Schedule 40 Female Adapter
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 female adapter with a socket (slip) end and a female pipe thread (FPT) end. Allows transitioning from a solvent-weld PVC system to accept a male-threaded fitting or pipe.
useCases: Accepting a male-threaded valve or fitting into a PVC system
Transitioning from PVC to threaded metal pipe
installationNotes: Solvent weld the socket end. Apply PTFE tape to the male threads of whatever is screwed into the FPT end. Do not over-tighten.

name: 3/4" PVC Schedule 40 90° Elbow SxS
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 90-degree elbow with socket × socket (SxS) connections. Changes pipe direction by 90 degrees. Joined by solvent welding with PVC primer and cement.
useCases: Standard direction change in irrigation and plumbing
installationNotes: Apply PVC primer to both pipe end and fitting socket. Then apply PVC cement and push together with a quarter turn. Hold for 10 seconds.

name: 1" × 3/4" PVC Schedule 40 Reducer Bushing
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 reducer bushing that steps down from 1" to 3/4". The hex-shaped body allows wrench grip for installation. Fits into a 1" fitting socket (spigot end) and accepts a 3/4" pipe (socket end).
useCases: Transitioning from 1" to 3/4" pipe within a fitting
Adapting different pipe sizes in irrigation systems
installationNotes: Apply PVC primer and cement. The hex shape allows using a wrench for installation if needed.

name: 1/2" PVC Schedule 40 Coupling FPTxFPT
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 coupling with female pipe thread (FPT) on both ends. Joins two male-threaded pipes or fittings together. No solvent welding — threaded connections only.
connectionType: Female Pipe Thread × Female Pipe Thread
useCases: Extending pipe runs with threaded connections
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to the male threads before screwing in. Hand-tight plus one turn with a wrench.

name: 1/2" PVC Schedule 40 Coupling SxS
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 coupling with socket (slip) connections on both ends. Joins two pieces of PVC pipe in a straight line using solvent welding.
useCases: Joining two pieces of PVC pipe in a straight run
Repairing a broken section of pipe
installationNotes: Apply PVC primer and cement to both pipe ends and both socket interiors. Push together with a quarter turn.

name: 1/2" PVC Schedule 40 90° Elbow SxS
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 90-degree elbow with socket × socket (SxS) connections in 1/2" size. Standard fitting for changing pipe direction by 90 degrees.
useCases: Standard plumbing and irrigation direction change
installationNotes: Apply PVC primer to both surfaces, then cement. Push together with a quarter turn and hold.

name: 3/4" PVC Schedule 40 45° Elbow SxS
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 45-degree elbow with socket × socket (SxS) connections. Creates a gentler direction change than a 90° elbow, reducing flow restriction.
useCases: Gradual direction changes in pipe runs
Drainage vent piping where gentle bends are needed
installationNotes: Apply PVC primer and cement. A 45° elbow causes less friction loss than a 90° elbow.

name: 3/4" PVC Schedule 40 Coupling SxS
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 coupling with socket (slip) connections on both ends in 3/4" size. Joins two pieces of 3/4" PVC pipe in a straight line using solvent welding.
useCases: Joining two pieces of 3/4" PVC pipe
installationNotes: Apply PVC primer and cement to both pipe ends and fitting sockets. Push together with a quarter turn.

name: 1/2" PVC Schedule 40 Riser Extension
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 riser extension (CPF 2103) with a male pipe thread (MPT) end and a female pipe thread (FPT) end. Used to extend threaded risers or stub-outs. The MPT end screws into an existing FPT fitting, and the FPT end accepts another MPT connection.
connectionType: Male Pipe Thread (MPT) × Female Pipe Thread (FPT)
useCases: Extending a threaded riser or stub-out
Adjusting the height of a water supply riser
Adding length to a threaded pipe run
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to the male threads before screwing in. Hand-tight plus one turn with a wrench.

name: 1-1/2" × 1/2" PVC Schedule 40 Reducer Bushing
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 reducer bushing that steps down from 1-1/2" to 1/2". The hex-shaped body allows wrench grip for installation. Fits into a 1-1/2" fitting socket (spigot end) and accepts a 1/2" pipe (socket end).
useCases: Connecting small branch lines to larger mains
installationNotes: Apply PVC primer and cement. The hex shape allows using a wrench for installation if needed.

name: 1-1/4" PVC Schedule 40 Male Adapter
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 male adapter (CPF 2109) with a socket (slip) end and a male pipe thread (MPT) end in 1-1/4" size. Transitions from solvent-weld PVC pipe to a threaded connection.
useCases: Connecting PVC pipe to a threaded valve or fitting
Transitioning from PVC to metal piping
installationNotes: Solvent weld the socket end to PVC pipe. Apply PTFE tape to the male threads before connecting to threaded fittings.

name: 1-1/2" × 2" PVC Schedule 40 Male Adapter Reducer
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 male adapter reducer (CPF 2110) that transitions from a 1-1/2" socket to a 2" male pipe thread. Combines reducing and adapting functions in one fitting.
useCases: Connecting a smaller PVC pipe to a larger threaded fitting
installationNotes: Solvent weld the socket end. Apply PTFE tape to the male threads. Price: ~$5.21.

name: 1-1/4" × 1" PVC Schedule 40 Reducer Bushing
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 reducer bushing (CPF 2107) that steps down from 1-1/4" to 1". Hex-shaped body allows wrench grip. Fits into a 1-1/4" fitting socket and accepts a 1" pipe.
installationNotes: Apply PVC primer and cement. The hex shape allows wrench grip. Price: ~$3.75.

name: 1-1/2" × 1-1/4" PVC Schedule 40 Reducer Bushing
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 reducer bushing that steps down from 1-1/2" to 1-1/4". Hex-shaped body for wrench grip. Fits into a 1-1/2" fitting socket and accepts 1-1/4" pipe.
useCases: Adapting different pipe sizes in a plumbing system
installationNotes: Apply PVC primer and cement to both surfaces.

name: 3/4" PVC Schedule 40 Tee SxSxS
description: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 tee with socket × socket × socket (SxSxS) connections in 3/4" size. Creates a T-shaped branch in a pipe run. All three ports accept 3/4" PVC pipe via solvent welding.
useCases: Creating branch connections in a pipe run
installationNotes: Apply PVC primer and cement to all three ports and pipe ends. Push together with a quarter turn.

name: 1-1/2" FerncoQuik Rubber Coupling
description: A FerncoQuik flexible rubber coupling (P1056-150) for 1-1/2" pipes. Features stainless steel band clamps for a secure, watertight connection. Rated for sewer, drain, and vent use only at 60 in. lbs torque.
compatibility: 1-1/2" PVC, cast iron, copper, steel, or lead pipe
installationNotes: Slide over pipe ends and tighten band clamps to 60 in. lbs. For sewer and drain use only — not rated for pressure.

name: 2" FerncoQuik Copper Coupling
description: A FerncoQuik copper-banded flexible coupling (P3001-22) for 2" pipes. Features stainless steel shielding and band clamps. Designed for connecting copper, PVC, cast iron, steel, or lead pipe.
connectionType: Band clamp compression with copper shielding
compatibility: 2" copper, PVC, cast iron, steel, or lead pipe
useCases: Repair couplings for drain and vent lines
installationNotes: Slide over pipe ends and tighten both band clamps evenly. Copper shielding provides superior rigidity over rubber-only couplings.

name: 1-1/2" No Hub Coupling
description: A 1-1/2" no hub coupling for joining hubless cast iron, PVC, or other drain pipe. Features a neoprene gasket with stainless steel shielding and band clamp for a secure, watertight seal.
useCases: Connecting cast iron to PVC in drain systems
Repair couplings for drain and vent stacks
installationNotes: Slide over pipe joint and tighten band clamp bolts evenly. Torque to manufacturer specifications. Price: ~$7.67.

name: 1-1/2" Fernco Shielded Flexible Coupling
description: A Fernco 1-1/2" × 1-1/2" shielded flexible coupling (P3002-150) with stainless steel shielding and band clamps. The shielded design provides more rigidity than standard rubber couplings. For sewer, drain and vent applications.
connectionType: Band clamp compression with steel shielding
compatibility: 1-1/2" PVC, cast iron, copper, steel, or lead pipe
useCases: Transition coupling between different pipe materials
Drain and vent connections requiring shielded support
installationNotes: Slide onto pipe ends and tighten both clamps evenly. Shielded design provides structural support in vertical stacks. Price: ~$1.63.

name: 2" Fernco Quick-Tee
description: A Fernco Quick-Tee (QT-200) flexible rubber tee fitting for 2" drain pipes. Features band clamps on all three ports for secure connections. Allows adding a branch connection to an existing drain line without cutting and gluing.
connectionType: Band clamp compression on all three ports
compatibility: 2" PVC, cast iron, copper, steel, or lead pipe
useCases: Adding a branch connection to an existing drain line
Tapping into a drain pipe without solvent welding
Connecting dissimilar pipe materials in a tee configuration
installationNotes: Slide onto pipes and tighten all three band clamps evenly. For sewer and drain use only — not rated for pressure.

name: 1-1/2" Fernco Flexible Elbow
description: A Fernco flexible elbow (PQL-150) for 1-1/2" drain pipes. Features rubber construction with stainless steel band clamps. Creates a 90° turn in a drain line without rigid PVC fittings.
compatibility: 1-1/2" PVC, cast iron, copper, steel, or lead pipe
useCases: Creating a flexible 90° turn in a drain line
Repair fittings where rigid elbows won't fit
installationNotes: Slide onto pipe ends and tighten both band clamps. Price: ~$10.85. For DWV use only.

name: 3" × 2" Fernco Flexible Coupling
description: A Fernco flexible reducing coupling (P1056-32) that transitions from 3" to 2" pipe. Features rubber construction with stainless steel band clamps. Connects different pipe sizes and materials.
compatibility: 2" pipe (small end) — PVC, cast iron, copper, steel, or lead
useCases: Reducing pipe size from 3" to 2" in drain lines
Connecting different pipe materials and sizes
installationNotes: Slide large end onto 3" pipe, small end onto 2" pipe. Tighten both band clamps. Price: ~$9.64.

name: 3" FerncoQuik Rubber Coupling
description: A FerncoQuik flexible rubber coupling (P1056-33) for 3" pipes. Features stainless steel band clamps for secure, watertight connections. For sewer, drain, and vent use.
compatibility: 3" PVC, cast iron, copper, steel, or lead pipe
useCases: Transition fittings between different 3" pipe materials
installationNotes: Slide over pipe ends and tighten both band clamps evenly. For sewer and drain use only.

name: 3/4" Brass Boiler Drain Valve
description: A 3/4" brass boiler drain valve with a blue quarter-turn handle and hose thread outlet. Features a female pipe thread (FPT) inlet connection and a male hose thread (MHT) outlet for connecting a garden hose.
connectionType: Female Pipe Thread (FPT) inlet × Male Hose Thread (MHT) outlet
useCases: Draining a boiler or water heater
Adding a hose connection to a pipe system
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to the male pipe thread. Screw into a 3/4" FPT fitting. Attach garden hose to the outlet when draining.

name: 3/4" Brass 3-Way Ball Valve
description: A 3/4" brass 3-way ball valve with a blue butterfly handle. Features three ports for diverting or mixing flow between two paths. Quarter-turn operation with threaded (FPT) connections.
compatibility: 3/4" MPT fittings on all three ports
useCases: Mixing hot and cold water lines
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to all threaded connections. Quarter-turn operation allows quick flow direction changes.

name: 3/4" Brass Ball Valve with Drain Port
description: A 3/4" brass ball valve with a lever handle and integrated drain port. Quarter-turn operation for full flow control. The drain port allows purging or draining the line downstream of the valve.
useCases: Main water shut-off with drain capability
installationNotes: Solder to copper pipe. Open the valve fully during soldering to prevent heat damage to the ball and seals.

name: 3/4" Everbilt Lead-Free Brass Ball Valve
description: A 3/4" Everbilt lead-free brass ball valve (150WSP/600WOG rated) with a blue lever handle. Solder × solder connections. Lead-free construction compliant with NSF/ANSI 372 for potable water use.
installationNotes: Solder to copper pipe with the valve in the fully open position. Rated 150WSP/600WOG.

name: 1" Everbilt Brass Ball Valve
description: A 1" Everbilt brass ball valve (107-455EB) with a blue lever handle. Solder × solder connections for copper pipe. Full port design for maximum flow.
installationNotes: Solder to copper pipe with valve in fully open position. Price: ~$24.68.

name: 1/4" × 2" Brass Pipe Nipple
description: A 1/4" MIP × 2" long brass pipe nipple (LFA-742). Male pipe threads on both ends for connecting two female-threaded fittings. Used for extending or connecting small-diameter threaded pipe runs.
connectionType: Male Iron Pipe (MIP) × Male Iron Pipe (MIP)
useCases: Extending air or gas line connections
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to both threaded ends before connecting.

name: 1/4" × 3" Brass Pipe Nipple
description: A 1/4" MIP × 3" long brass pipe nipple (LFA-744). Male pipe threads on both ends. Longer than the 2" version for applications requiring more spacing between threaded connections.
connectionType: Male Iron Pipe (MIP) × Male Iron Pipe (MIP)
useCases: Connecting threaded fittings with more spacing
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to both threaded ends.

name: 3/8" × 2-1/2" Brass Pipe Nipple
description: A 3/8" MIP × 2-1/2" long brass pipe nipple (LFA-788). Male pipe threads on both ends. Used for connecting threaded fittings in small-diameter pipe runs.
connectionType: Male Iron Pipe (MIP) × Male Iron Pipe (MIP)
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to both threaded ends.

name: 3/8" × 3" Brass Pipe Nipple
description: A 3/8" MIP × 3" long brass pipe nipple (LFA-789). Male pipe threads on both ends.
connectionType: Male Iron Pipe (MIP) × Male Iron Pipe (MIP)
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to both threaded ends.

name: 3/4" × 2" Brass Pipe Nipple
description: A 3/4" MIP × 2" long brass pipe nipple (LFA-879). Male pipe threads on both ends for connecting larger threaded fittings.
connectionType: Male Iron Pipe (MIP) × Male Iron Pipe (MIP)
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to both threaded ends. Price: ~$6.37.

name: 3/4" × 4" Brass Pipe Nipple
description: A 3/4" MIP × 4" long brass pipe nipple (LFA-889). Male pipe threads on both ends. Longer version for greater spacing between fittings.
connectionType: Male Iron Pipe (MIP) × Male Iron Pipe (MIP)
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to both threaded ends. Price: ~$15.67.

name: 1/2" × Close Brass Pipe Nipple
description: A 1/2" MIP × close brass pipe nipple (LFA-833). Close nipples have threads running nearly the full length with minimal unthreaded body. Used to connect two FPT fittings with minimal spacing.
connectionType: Male Iron Pipe (MIP) × Male Iron Pipe (MIP)
useCases: Connecting two threaded fittings with minimal gap
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to both ends. Close nipples have very little wrench surface — use two wrenches when tightening.

name: 1/2" × 2" Brass Pipe Nipple
description: A 1/2" MIP × 2" long brass pipe nipple. Male pipe threads on both ends for standard threaded connections.
connectionType: Male Iron Pipe (MIP) × Male Iron Pipe (MIP)
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to both threaded ends. Price: ~$13.97.

name: 3/4" × Close Brass Pipe Nipple
description: A 3/4" MIP × close brass pipe nipple (LFA-877). Close nipples have threads running nearly the full length. Connects two 3/4" FPT fittings with minimal spacing.
connectionType: Male Iron Pipe (MIP) × Male Iron Pipe (MIP)
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape. Use two wrenches — close nipples have minimal wrench surface. Price: ~$10.81.

name: 1" × Close Brass Pipe Nipple
description: A 1" MIP × close brass pipe nipple (LFA-911). Close nipples connect two 1" FPT fittings with minimal spacing between them.
connectionType: Male Iron Pipe (MIP) × Male Iron Pipe (MIP)
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to both ends. Use back-wrench technique.

name: 1" × 3/4" Brass Reducing Bushing
description: A 1" MIP × 3/4" FIP brass reducing bushing (LFA-878). Steps down from a 1" female fitting to accept a 3/4" male threaded connection. Hex-shaped for wrench grip.
connectionType: Male Iron Pipe (MIP) 1" × Female Iron Pipe (FIP) 3/4"
useCases: Reducing pipe size from 1" to 3/4" in threaded systems
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to the male threads. Use wrench on hex body. Price: ~$5.67.

name: 3/4" Viega ProPress Coupling
description: A 3/4" Viega ProPress coupling for copper pipe. Features copper construction with integrated EPDM sealing elements and Smart Connect technology. Press connections on both ends eliminate the need for soldering.
compatibility: 3/4" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
useCases: Repair work in occupied buildings (no open flame)
installationNotes: Use Viega-approved pressing tool with appropriate jaw. Ensure pipe ends are deburred and marked for insertion depth. Smart Connect feature leaks during pressure test if not pressed.

name: 3/4" Viega ProPress Adapter
description: A 3/4" Viega ProPress adapter (AD-02, Part #77995) with a press connection on one end and male pipe thread (MPT) on the other. Transitions from ProPress copper system to threaded fittings.
useCases: Transitioning from ProPress to threaded connections
Connecting to threaded valves or equipment
installationNotes: Press one end with Viega-approved tool. Apply PTFE tape to threaded end.

name: 3/4" Brass 90° FPT Elbow
description: A 3/4" brass 90° elbow with female pipe thread (FPT) connections on both ends. Creates a right-angle turn in a threaded pipe run.
connectionType: Female Pipe Thread (FPT) × Female Pipe Thread (FPT)
useCases: Creating 90° turns in threaded pipe systems
installationNotes: Use with brass or galvanized pipe nipples. Apply PTFE tape to male threads.

name: 1/2" Brass Reducing Adapter MIP × MIP
description: A brass reducing adapter with male pipe threads (MIP) on both ends in different sizes. Used to connect two female-threaded fittings of different diameters.
connectionType: Male Iron Pipe (MIP) × Male Iron Pipe (MIP)
useCases: Reducing pipe diameter in threaded systems
installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to both threaded ends.

name: 1/2" Viega ProPress Coupling
description: A 1/2" Viega ProPress coupling for copper pipe. Features copper construction with EPDM sealing elements and Smart Connect technology for flameless connections.
compatibility: 1/2" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
installationNotes: Use Viega-approved pressing tool. Deburr pipe ends and mark insertion depth before pressing.

name: 1/2" Viega ProPress 90° Elbow
description: A 1/2" Viega ProPress 90° elbow for copper pipe. Creates a right-angle turn using press connections — no soldering required. Features EPDM sealing elements.
compatibility: 1/2" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
useCases: Creating 90° turns in copper pipe without soldering
installationNotes: Use Viega-approved pressing tool with appropriate jaw size. Deburr and mark pipe before inserting.

name: 1/2" Viega ProPress Street Elbow
description: A 1/2" Viega ProPress 90° street elbow with a press connection on one end and a fitting (FTG) insertion end on the other. The FTG end inserts directly into another press fitting, saving space.
useCases: Connecting directly into another press fitting
installationNotes: Press the standard end with Viega tool. FTG end inserts into the cup of another fitting. No additional coupling needed.

name: 1/2" Viega ProPress Tee
description: A 1/2" Viega ProPress tee (TE-03, Part #77237) for ASTM B88 copper tubing. Features press connections on all three ports. Creates a branch connection without soldering.
compatibility: 1/2" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
useCases: Creating branch connections in copper pipe
installationNotes: Use Viega-approved pressing tool on all three connections. Deburr pipe ends and mark insertion depth.

name: 1/2" Viega ProPress Ball Valve
description: A 1/2" Viega ProPress ball valve with press connections on both ends. Full-port brass ball valve with lever handle. No soldering required for installation.
compatibility: 1/2" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
installationNotes: Press both connections with Viega-approved tool. Valve must be in open position during pressing.

name: 3/4" × 1/2" Viega ProPress Reducing Coupling
description: A Viega ProPress reducing coupling that transitions from 3/4" to 1/2" copper pipe. Press connections on both ends. Eliminates the need for soldering when reducing pipe size.
useCases: Reducing pipe size from 3/4" to 1/2" in copper systems
installationNotes: Use appropriate jaw size for each end. Press the 3/4" side with 3/4" jaws and the 1/2" side with 1/2" jaws.

name: 3/4" Viega ProPress 90° Elbow
description: A 3/4" Viega ProPress 90° elbow for copper pipe. Creates a right-angle turn using press connections — no soldering required.
compatibility: 3/4" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
installationNotes: Use Viega-approved pressing tool with 3/4" jaws. Deburr and mark insertion depth.

name: 3/4" Viega ProPress Tee
description: A 3/4" Viega ProPress tee for copper pipe. Press connections on all three ports allow creating branch connections without soldering.
compatibility: 3/4" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
useCases: Creating branch connections in 3/4" copper pipe
installationNotes: Use Viega-approved pressing tool with 3/4" jaws on all three connections.

name: 3/4" × 3/4" × 1/2" Viega ProPress Reducing Tee
description: A Viega ProPress reducing tee with 3/4" run connections and a 1/2" branch. Allows branching off a smaller line from a 3/4" main without soldering.
useCases: Branching a 1/2" fixture line from a 3/4" main
Reducing tee connections in copper systems
installationNotes: Use 3/4" jaws on the run connections and 1/2" jaws on the branch connection.

name: 3/4" Viega ProPress Ball Valve
description: A 3/4" Viega ProPress ball valve (BV-03, Part #77415) with press connections on both ends. Full-port brass ball valve with lever handle. No soldering required.
compatibility: 3/4" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
installationNotes: Press both connections with Viega-approved tool. Keep valve fully open during pressing. Price: ~$22+.

name: 1" Viega ProPress 45° Elbow
description: A 1" Viega ProPress 45° elbow (EL-06, Part #77275) for ASTM B88 copper tubing. Creates a 45° direction change using press connections.
compatibility: 1" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
useCases: Gentle direction changes in main lines
installationNotes: Use Viega-approved pressing tool with 1" jaws. Price: ~$19.76.

name: 1" Viega ProPress Coupling
description: A 1" Viega ProPress coupling for copper pipe. Press connections on both ends for flameless joining of 1" copper pipe.
compatibility: 1" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
installationNotes: Use Viega-approved pressing tool with 1" jaws. Deburr pipe ends.

name: 1" Viega ProPress 90° Elbow
description: A 1" Viega ProPress 90° elbow for copper pipe. Creates a right-angle turn in 1" copper pipe using press connections.
compatibility: 1" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
useCases: Creating 90° turns in 1" copper main lines
installationNotes: Use Viega-approved pressing tool with 1" jaws.

name: 1" Viega ProPress Tee
description: A 1" Viega ProPress tee for copper pipe. Press connections on all three ports for creating branch connections without soldering.
compatibility: 1" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
useCases: Creating branch connections in 1" copper pipe
installationNotes: Use Viega-approved pressing tool with 1" jaws on all three connections.

name: 1" × 1" × 1/2" Viega ProPress Reducing Tee
description: A Viega ProPress reducing tee with 1" run connections and a 1/2" branch port. Allows branching a smaller fixture line from a 1" main.
useCases: Branching a 1/2" fixture line from a 1" main
Fixture supply tap-offs from main lines
installationNotes: Use 1" jaws on run connections and 1/2" jaws on branch.

name: Weller Lead-Free Solid Core Plumbing Solder
description: Weller lead-free solid core plumbing solder, 4oz spool, 0.118"/3mm diameter. Melting point 440-599°F. NSF certified for potable water connections.
connectionType: Used with soldering torch for sweat connections
installationNotes: Apply flux before soldering. Heat the fitting, not the solder. Solder will flow toward the heat source via capillary action.

name: Oatey No.5 Lead Free Solder Paste Flux
description: Oatey No.5 lead-free solder paste flux, 8 oz jar. Used to clean and prepare copper surfaces for soldering. NSF certified for potable water use.
connectionType: Applied to copper surfaces before soldering
useCases: Cleaning oxidation from pipe and fittings
Ensuring proper solder flow in joints
installationNotes: Apply thin, even coat to both pipe exterior and fitting interior before assembling. Clean excess flux after soldering.

name: 3/4" Nibco Copper Coupling
description: A 3/4" Nibco copper coupling (C × C) for joining two lengths of copper pipe. Standard solder/sweat connection.
compatibility: 3/4" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
useCases: Joining two lengths of 3/4" copper pipe
installationNotes: Clean and flux both pipe ends and coupling interior. Solder with lead-free solder.

name: 3/4" Copper 90° Street Elbow
description: A 3/4" copper 90° street elbow (607-2) with a fitting (FTG) end that inserts into another fitting cup and a solder cup on the other end. Saves space by eliminating the need for a short piece of pipe.
installationNotes: Clean and flux both surfaces. The FTG end inserts directly into another fitting cup. Solder both joints.

name: 3/4" Copper 90° Elbow
description: A 3/4" copper 90° elbow (607) with solder cup connections on both ends (C × C). Standard fitting for making right-angle turns in copper pipe.
compatibility: 3/4" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
installationNotes: Clean and flux both pipe and fitting. Solder with lead-free solder. Apply heat to the fitting, not the solder.

name: 3/4" Copper Female Adapter
description: A 3/4" copper female adapter (C × FPT) that transitions from a solder connection to a female pipe thread. Accepts 3/4" MPT threaded connections.
useCases: Transitioning from copper to threaded connections
installationNotes: Solder the C end to copper pipe. Use PTFE tape on male threads inserted into the FPT end.

name: 1" Everbilt Copper Union
description: A 1" Everbilt copper union (C633W, C × C) that allows disconnection of a pipe joint without cutting. Features a brass threaded nut for easy assembly/disassembly.
compatibility: 1" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
installationNotes: Solder each half to the pipe separately. Assemble with union nut. Can be disassembled for equipment service.

name: 1" × 3/4" Copper Reducing Coupling
description: A 1" × 3/4" copper reducing coupling (Everbilt #857041, C × C) that transitions from 1" to 3/4" copper pipe. Solder connections on both ends.
installationNotes: Clean and flux both pipe ends and coupling interior. Solder both joints.

name: 1" Copper Male Adapter
description: A 1" copper male adapter (C × MPT) that transitions from a solder connection to a male pipe thread connection.
useCases: Transitioning from copper to threaded connections
installationNotes: Solder the C end. Apply PTFE tape to the MPT threads before threading into a female connection.

name: 3/4" Copper Male Adapter
description: A 3/4" copper male adapter (C × MIP) made by Everbilt (#497903). Transitions from solder cup to male pipe thread. Available in 10-packs.
installationNotes: Solder the C end. Apply PTFE tape to MPT threads.

name: 1" Copper 45° Elbow
description: A 1" copper 45° elbow with solder cup connections on both ends (C × C). Creates a gentle 45° direction change in copper pipe.
compatibility: 1" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
installationNotes: Clean and flux both surfaces. Solder with lead-free solder.

name: 3/4" Copper Tee
description: A 3/4" copper tee with solder cup connections on all three ports (C × C × C). Creates a branch connection in copper pipe runs.
compatibility: 3/4" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
installationNotes: Clean and flux all three joints. Solder all connections. Heat each joint individually.

name: 3/4" Nibco Copper Tube Strap
description: 3/4" Nibco copper tube straps (CTS) for mounting copper pipe to framing. Two-hole design for screw mounting. Sold in multi-packs.
useCases: Securing copper pipes to wall studs or joists
installationNotes: Use copper-compatible screws. Space per local code requirements (typically every 6-8 feet for horizontal runs).

name: 1/2" Copper 90° Elbow
description: A 1/2" Everbilt copper 90° elbow (C607, C × C) with solder cup connections on both ends. Standard fitting for 1/2" copper pipe direction changes.
compatibility: 1/2" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
installationNotes: Clean and flux both surfaces. Solder with lead-free solder.

name: 3/4" × 3/4" × 1/2" Copper Reducing Tee
description: A 3/4" × 3/4" × 1/2" Everbilt copper reducing tee (#187526) with solder connections. 3/4" run with a 1/2" branch port for stepping down to smaller fixture supply lines.
installationNotes: Clean and flux all three joints. Solder each connection individually.

name: 1" Copper 90° Elbow
description: A 1" copper 90° elbow (607) with solder cup connections on both ends (C × C). Standard fitting for making right-angle turns in 1" copper pipe.
compatibility: 1" Type K, L, or M copper pipe
useCases: Creating 90° turns in 1" copper main lines
Direction changes in main water supply
installationNotes: Clean and flux both surfaces. Solder with lead-free solder. 1" fittings require more heat than smaller sizes.
